title: When All Doors Are Closed
body:
When you’re in pain, ask yourself:
Is your problem bigger than the one in the belly of a fish?
Bigger than the one left in a dark well?
Bigger than the one thrown into fire?
Bigger than the one with an army behind and the sea ahead?

No. They are not.

Your problems may be hard, but you can still imagine a way out.
But what does science say about surviving inside a fish, a well, or the sea?

A doctor once said, and he was right:
True faith is when you can’t see the way, but you’re still at peace, knowing He will handle it.

He, above whom there is nothing.
He, by whose will everything happens.

One Name: *Musabbib-ul-Asbaab* — The One who creates the causes.
For us, rain needs clouds. But He is the One who made that rule.

Have faith. He will take care.
He got you through those dark, shaking nights. He will get you through what comes next too.
He hears you. He sees you. He knows everything.

What He decreed will happen.
What He didn’t decree will never happen.

*So what do we do? Where do we go? What decision is right?*
These questions tear us apart.

Just pray.
Keep trying while staying within Allah’s limits.
Don’t lose hope.

Whether it goes your way or not, the destination is already set by Him.
Want to change it? Pray. If He wills, He will. If not, His decision is best.

In this short life, even getting everything you want won’t be enough.
Be content with how He keeps you.

Maybe this all sounds like “just words.”
But what feels like just words, aren’t always just words.

I’m with you. I pray for you.
excerpt: A heartfelt reminder about faith in hard times. It compares our struggles to the trials of the Prophets and says no hardship is bigger than theirs. The core message: trust Allah even when you see no way out. He is _Musabbib-ul-Asbaab_ — The Creator of causes. Keep praying, keep trying, and accept that His plan is always better than ours.

title: Light Finds a Way
caption: Even when the day is ending, the sun still finds gaps to shine through.

title: Decay & What Remains
body:
Let me unfold the meaning of Umar—the quiet measure of a life.
Will you understand?
A leaf is born as a shy promise, a tender bud.
It swells within the green womb of the branch, shaping its identity.
Only the one who longs to know truly sees it—watching closely, with a yearning heart.
Then comes the moment of emergence: a gentle push, a breaking forth.
A small, innocent child-leaf appears—soft, fragile, so delicate that even a breeze might bruise it.
Slowly it grows into its youth, its most beautiful age.
Strong, vibrant, full of life—
dancing when the wind brushes against it,
whispering secrets in every rustle,
offering shade to the weary, standing tall in its original colour.
Then time pulls it toward old age.
Its hue begins to fade, its voice grows thin, its strength quietly ebbs.
First, the soul inside withers.
Its bond with the branch weakens.
It accepts, with quiet grace, that its season is ending.
Yellow creeps in like the first touch of twilight.
It begins to curl and wither.
Its grip loosens.
One day, it breaks free and falls.
Down it drifts to the earth.
There it crumbles, piece by piece, scattering like forgotten dust,
until only a delicate skeleton remains—
like the bare bones of a human frame.
And then, slowly, even that frame dissolves into the soil.
Dissolution—perhaps you understand this word better than I.
In the end, it vanishes.
But… your science says matter never truly ends.
Matter—did I use the right word?
These days my Urdu feels rusty.
I hesitate over every line I write, checking if the words are worthy, if the sentences flow.
Often I delete everything in fear.
Yet today I did not check.
Because I know you understand—even through my flaws.
The leaf does not truly disappear.
It remains somewhere—in the soil, in the air, in another form.
As I wrote this, I remembered the flower I once scattered across the mountains.
Its remains must still exist somewhere—in some shape, in some hidden place.
When our own stories fade,
we too will remain somewhere, in some form.
Our sighs, our tears, our pain-filled breaths—
they will linger, somewhere, in some way…
🤍
excerpt: Umar — the quiet journey of a life. A leaf is born as a tender bud, grows into a soft innocent child, reaches its most beautiful and strong youth, then slowly fades into old age. It withers, falls, crumbles, and dissolves into the earth. Yet, science reminds us that matter never truly disappears — it only changes form. Like the leaf, our stories, sighs, tears, and very being will also linger somew
